item.title: World Cup final draws record 62.8M U.S. viewers
item.description:
Sunday's FIFA World Cup final drew more than 62.8 million viewers in the U.S., making it the most-watched World Cup final and soccer telecast in American history.

Why it matters: The record audience underscores soccer's growing popularity in the U.S. and is likely to boost the value of future media rights.

By the numbers: The game, which saw Spain beat defending champion Argentina 1–0 in extra time, also was the most-watched soccer match in Spanish-language media history, Telemundo said.

  • Nearly 39 million U.S. viewers watched the game on Fox, which had exclusive English-language broadcast rights in the U.S. Those figures are up 132% from the 2022 final on Fox, the company said.
  • Another nearly 24 million U.S. viewers tuned into the broadcast on Telemundo, which had exclusive Spanish-language broadcast rights in the U.S. Those figures are up 166% from the 2022 final on Telemundo.
Data: Nielsen; Note: Includes English and Spanish broadcasts. Nielsen began including out-of-home and streaming viewing in its official national TV ratings in 2020; Chart: Kerry Flynn/Axios

Streaming played a big role in the tournament's reach. June was the biggest subscriber acquisition month for Fox's streaming service, Fox One, according to Antenna.

  • Fox One added an estimated 2.8 million new subscribers in June, more than 2.5 times its previous monthly record in January during the NFL playoffs, per Antenna.
  • Nearly all of Fox's new June subscribers — 93% — were first-time customers rather than returning users.

The intrigue: Peacock, which streams Telemundo, also saw strong subscriber growth, recording an estimated 3.75 million U.S. sign-ups in June, per Antenna.

  • Streaming accounted for nearly half of Telemundo's World Cup audience, up from 30% in 2022, per Telemundo.
  • Telemundo's coverage resonated beyond Spanish speakers, as some preferred the energetic commentary, lack of commercials during the hydration breaks and cheaper cost of Peacock compared to Fox One.

Between the lines: America hosting the tournament, a competitive U.S. men's national team and broader streaming distribution helped push viewership to new heights in the U.S.

  • The U.S. team's matches set audience records. Fox said the U.S. opening match against Paraguay on June 12 became the most-watched FIFA Men's World Cup telecast in English-language U.S. history, averaging 18 million viewers.
  • The U.S. men's victory over Bosnia and Herzegovina on July 1 set the record as the biggest English-language TV audience for any soccer match in America with 26.4 million viewers on Fox.
  • The U.S. team's loss to Belgium on July 6 later bested the record with 37.2 million viewers on Fox, per Nielsen, along with 12.9 million viewers on Telemundo, for more than 50 million combined.
  • The July 5 match between Mexico and England came close to that record with 46.7 million viewers on Fox and Telemundo.

The big picture: The FIFA World Cup has historically attracted the largest global audience of any recurring television event worldwide.

  • FIFA has not yet released worldwide figures. The 2022 World Cup final between Argentina and France held the previous final game viewership record at 1.42 billion worldwide, per FIFA.
  • Live sports remain one of the few programming categories capable of attracting massive live audiences, driving both advertising revenue and streaming subscriptions.
  • FIFA also experimented with bringing more American-style entertainment to the event, including its first halftime show for a World Cup final, to help the tournament appeal beyond traditional soccer fans.

What to watch: FIFA is expected to cash in on the tournament's success by opening up the media rights bidding process for the 2030 World Cup.

  • Fox and Telemundo's current agreements ran through the 2026 tournament, after FIFA extended the deals without a competitive bidding process for the 2022 tournament.
  • FIFA has reportedly alerted media companies that English- and Spanish-language U.S. rights are likely to be sold together as a package for 2030, per CNBC, with Netflix, Disney and YouTube all interested in bidding.

item.title: Scoop: Lawmaker asks SEC to investigate Truth Social's plan to monetize access to its data
item.description:
Rep. Ritchie Torres (D-N.Y.) has asked the Securities and Exchange Commission to investigate whether plans by Truth Social's parent company to sell Wall Street firms real-time access to President Trump's posts could violate federal securities laws, according to a copy of the letter obtained by Axios.

Why it matters: The letter escalates scrutiny of Trump Media & Technology Group's plan to monetize faster access for institutional investors to President Trump's market-moving social posts.

Catch up quick: TMTG's new Truth API product, which is slated to debut Aug. 1, will provide real-time access to publicly available posts from the platform's top 10 trending accounts, Axios reported last week.

  • TMTG executives reportedly pitched the service for a licensing fee of up to $100,000 per month, per Reuters.
  • In the future, the API can be expanded to more accounts for customers willing to pay more, interim CEO Kevin McGurn told Axios.

Zoom in: In the letter sent Monday to SEC chair Paul Atkins, Torres urged the agency to review Truth API before its planned launch and determine whether it raises securities law, market manipulation or investor protection concerns.

  • "There is a fundamental difference between a social media platform licensing ordinary data and a company substantially owned by the sitting President selling sophisticated traders faster access to his market-moving policy announcements," the letter reads, while noting the president reportedly retains a roughly 41% stake in the company through a revocable trust.
  • "Presidential communications capable of affecting stocks, bonds, oil, currencies, and other assets should not become a premium financial product that gives wealthy institutions a millisecond advantage over retail investors," it continues.
  • "Even a brief informational edge can generate substantial profits in algorithmic markets and erode confidence in a fair and level playing field."

The other side: A TMTG spokesperson told Axios, "Truth API offers customers the fastest way to ingest publicly available Truth Social data."

Between the lines: Torres also suggested the SEC coordinate with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, the Office of Government Ethics and other appropriate federal entities to scrutinize any potential conflicts of interest.

  • He asked the SEC to disclose whether it is reviewing the product and what safeguards will be in place to detect suspicious trading around presidential posts.

Zoom out: Torres' letter is the latest in a string of concern from both sides of the aisle around the product.

  • Last week, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said "Truth API is the definition of insider trading."
  • On Tuesday, Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters on Capitol Hill, "My assumption is that'll have some regulatory look — legal look. My assumption is that'll get tested somewhere. I don't know the answer to that. It's kind of plowing new ground, new territory in these predictive markets."

The big picture: The SEC has previously probed TMTG's accounting firm and the SPAC company that merged with TMTG.

  • The agency settled with the SPAC, Digital World Acquisition Corporation, in 2023.
  • TMTG stock has rallied in recent weeks on optimism around its business model expansion, but it remains well below its 2024 post-SPAC merger peak.

Disclosure: In 2023, TMTG sued 20 media organizations, including Axios, for defamation. Litigation is ongoing.

item.title: Scoop: Trump's top MAGA advisers gather to plot midterms
item.description:
President Trump's top political advisers inside and outside of the White House are meeting Tuesday afternoon to review polling, their messaging and strategy ahead of the Nov. 3 midterms.

Why it matters: Trump's super PAC and the Republican National Committee are sitting on about $530 million, and now his team is figuring out how, when and where to deploy it.

  • That doesn't include what insiders say is hundreds of millions of dollars more in Trump's dark-money political nonprofit, Securing American Greatness.

Zoom in: The meeting at the Four Seasons hotel in D.C. is the second of what are becoming regular Trump team conclaves to coordinate the GOP, its candidates and his political operation. Trump won't be there, but these top MAGA shot-callers will be:

  • Tony Fabrizio, Trump's longtime top pollster, who will give a presentation about the midterm picture.
  • James Blair, Trump's hands-on political operations chief in charge of executing a midterm plan.
  • Susie Wiles, White House chief of staff and Trump's longest-serving political adviser.
  • Chris LaCivita, Trump's former campaign co-manager with Wiles.
  • Joe Gruters, RNC chair under Trump and a longtime ally of Wiles.
  • Cabinet officials (except Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who's in Manila on official business).

Zoom out: Republicans acknowledge it will be tough to maintain control of the U.S. House with Trump's poll numbers bottoming out amid rising gas prices and the war in Iran.

  • But Trump's political operatives say voters are wary of Democrats' progressive overreach, and they think this election will be about who Americans hate more, not who they love.
  • Republicans also sparked an unprecedented round of mid-decade redistricting that could net the GOP 10 additional House seats, making that chamber easier to defend.

Yes, but: Trump is fighting political gravity. With few exceptions, a president whose party controls both chambers of Congress usually loses control of at least one in a midterm election.

  • Polls show voters don't believe the economy is in good shape and are blaming Trump.
  • "Voters feel that Trump's economic policies have made things worse — because they have," John Anzalone, a top Democratic pollster who also surveys for the Wall Street Journal, wrote last week on X.

What they're saying: "The Trump tax cuts have saved people real money and the president is keeping Americans safe," said one Trump operative involved in Tuesday's meeting.

  • "We have the manpower, the message and the money to make that clear."

item.title: Scoop: Trump's Cabinet members are joining TikTok after DOJ shift
item.description:
Most of President Trump's Cabinet will launch TikTok accounts Tuesday, a move that comes days after the Justice Department told federal employees it was safe to use the app because of its sale to a U.S.-approved owner.

Why it matters: The move is a striking shift from Trump's first term, when his administration argued that TikTok posed a national security threat because of its ownership by China's ByteDance.

  • By joining the app themselves, Cabinet officials are effectively signaling that TikTok is now an acceptable venue for government messaging.

The background: The 2022 No TikTok on Government Devices Act said TikTok couldn't be used on government-issued phones.

  • But Trump's DOJ now says that law is no longer applicable, given the change in the company's ownership to a U.S.-controlled venture earlier year.

Zoom in: Trump's administration now views TikTok as a powerful tool for reaching young voters.

  • Trump himself has been on the app since 2024, and some of his posts — including one documenting his appearance at a UFC event — have gotten hundreds of millions of views.

Zoom in: Trump's administration now views TikTok as a powerful tool for reaching young voters.

item.title: Mike Johnson backs ban on lawmakers dating Hill staff
item.description:
House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) told Axios Tuesday he supports banning members of Congress from having romantic or sexual relationships with Capitol Hill staff, adding momentum to a discussion about tightening House workplace rules.

Why it matters: Congress has been rocked this year by a series of sexual misconduct scandals, prompting bipartisan conversations about reforming how the House deals with sexual harassment and misconduct.

  • "I think it's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace," Johnson told Axios when asked about lawmakers engaging in relationships with staffers.
  • "We should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America, so that's an easy one for me," Johnson continued.
  • House rules prohibit members from engaging in sexual relationships with members of their own staff, but do not prohibit relationships with staff members outside their offices.

Driving the news: A bipartisan House task force on sexual misconduct is weighing whether to recommend banning lawmaker-staffer relations.

  • "If you're a member, you really should not be doing that because you have extraordinary power and authority over that individual's career, even if they are not in your office," Rep. Kat Cammack (R-Fla.), the GOP lead on the task force, told Axios Tuesday.
  • "There is a massive power dynamic imbalance," Cammack added.

"From the beginning, we've pushed to extend the prohibition against members having sexual relationships to ALL staff," the task force's Democratic lead, Rep. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.), said in a statement to Axios.

  • "The power dynamic that leads to abuse exists for all staffers, not just those within the members own office," she continued.

Cammack said banning relationships with staff outside of a member's office remains under discussion, though lawmakers have a "difference of opinion" on the proposal.

  • She said the task force is "70% of the way there" on a broader package of recommendations.

Catch up quick: Calls for tougher rules ramped up after two lawmakers — Reps. Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) and Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) — resigned earlier this year amid allegations of sexual misconduct. Swalwell also faces allegations of sexual assault.

  • Swalwell has denied the allegations, and Gonzales admitted to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide.
  • The Ethics panel lost jurisdiction over the matters when each of the lawmakers stepped down. Both were staring down imminent expulsion votes.

Separately, Axios has reported that Rep. Chuck Edwards (R-N.C.) had singled out two young female staffers for special attention that sources described as inappropriate.

  • Edwards is under House Ethics investigation for alleged sexual harassment and creating a hostile work environment.
  • Rep. Cory Mills (R-Fla.) is also under House Ethics investigation for allegations that center on domestic abuse, stolen valor and financial misconduct, all of which he denies.

Zoom in: The New York Post reported that Senator Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) engaged in romantic relationships with two staffers outside of his office while he served in the House. Asked about the allegations, Gallego told NBC News that he was "not going to engage in gossip."

  • In response, House Conference Chairwoman Lisa McClain (R-Mich.), the highest-ranking woman in GOP leadership, called for a ban on member relations with congressional staff.
  • "Even if a relationship is consensual, there is an inherent power imbalance that can undermine trust and create an appearance of impropriety," McClain wrote on X.

Editor's note: This story has been updated with comments from Rep. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.).

item.title: Trump vows U.S. will soon bomb Iran's Pickaxe Mountain
item.description:
President Trump said Tuesday that the U.S. military will bomb Iran's Pickaxe Mountain, a deep underground site that the U.S. and Israel suspect could be used for nuclear activity.

Why it matters: Trump's remarks, at the top of his meeting with the president of Lebanon at the White House, signaled that he is not interested in a ceasefire at the moment and is moving towards escalating the strikes on Iran.

What he's saying: "We'll be hitting that area very probably pretty soon. There's not a thing they can do about it. You know, normally I wouldn't say that. If I thought they could do something about it, I would never say that. But we'll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily," Trump said.

  • Trump said the U.S. thinks Iran "may have" stored centrifuges at the site but added, "We don't have it on record." Even if Iran did, "it doesn't mean anything unless they have the [nuclear] material." He said Iran does not.
  • The Wall Street Journal reported on Monday that Israel provided the U.S. with intelligence suggesting that Iran has moved thousands of centrifuges into Pickaxe Mountain.

Yes, but: It is not clear whether the U.S. military has bombs that can penetrate the rock above the deep underground facility. Nevertheless, a U.S. strike could do damage to the site and block its entrance tunnels.

Driving the news: U.S. officials say Trump is nearing a fork in the Iran war, with two options he is considering.

  • Option 1: Pursue a new 10-day ceasefire aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Qatar, Egypt, Pakistan and other regional mediators are urging him to do so.
  • Option 2: Launch a massive joint military campaign with Israel to try to force Tehran to capitulate.

State of play: Trump signaled on Tuesday he is leaning toward the second choice. He expressed no interest in restarting negotiations with Iran.

  • "They want to desperately meet, and until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest," he said.
  • Trump stressed the U.S. is "not finished at all" with the war. Iran, he said, hasn't "seen anything yet. We have been nice."

  • Option 2: Launch a massive joint military campaign with Israel to try to force Tehran to capitulate.

State of play: Trump signaled on Tuesday he is leaning toward the second choice. He expressed no interest in restarting negotiations with Iran.

  • "They want to desperately meet, and until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest," he said.
  • Trump stressed the U.S. is "not finished at all" with the war. Iran, he said, hasn't "seen anything yet. We have been nice."
item.dc:creator: Barak Ravid

media:content.media:description: Lebanese President Joseph Aoun meets with President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday. (Aaron Schwartz/CNP/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

item.title: Trump admin freezes more than $1 billion in Medicaid funds
item.description:
The Trump administration is halting more than $1 billion in federal Medicaid payments to California and Minnesota until the states take more steps to investigate questionable claims, federal health officials said on Tuesday.

Why it matters: The moves continue a series of freezes to blue states' funding as part of the administration's crackdown on fraud.

  • They also maintain scrutiny of Minnesota's safety net funding after the administration used the state's inability to rein in fraud as justification for launching last year's ICE enforcement surge.

Driving the news: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services said it's deferring about $867.5 million in Medicaid payments to California and $199 million to Minnesota after financial reviews turned up unsupported claims for in-home care and other services.

  • The Department of Health and Human Services said it also will expand its authority to remove bad actors from federal health programs and possibly bar them from returning.
  • CMS said it reviewed California's claims for certain in-home care programs after identifying spending growth that exceeded national trends and other claims officials said require more documentation.
  • The agency also cited Medicaid claims in 14 service areas in Minnesota that it said require additional documentation, including expenditures linked to providers who had been flagged through program integrity reviews.

"CMS is done trying to chase down stolen and misused funds after they've already left the building," said CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz, adding the moves would safeguard federal taxpayer dollars.

Context: In May, CMS announced it would defer $1.3 billion in Medicaid funds to California, in what Oz said at the time was the biggest deferral ever made by the agency.

  • Officials also said they were imposing a six-month moratorium on enrolling new hospice and home health providers in Medicare, which it identified as key areas of fraudulent activity.

This is a developing story. Check here for updates.

item.title: Democrats probe Epstein's ties with foreign governments
item.description:
Convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein may have acted as an unregistered foreign agent — and tried to leverage his past relationship with President Trump, when doing so, the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee alleged in a letter demanding records.

The big picture: Rep. Jamie Raskin's (D-Md.) inquiry previews the types of investigations the party could launch with subpoena power if they retake a chamber this fall.

Driving the news: Raskin wrote to administration officials that the Epstein files show the disgraced financier seemingly tried to become an emissary for Saudi Arabia and appeared to advise leaders in Kuwait, Israel and Russia.

  • In one exchange, Epstein appeared to pitch himself to Saudi Arabia as a "Financial Confidant to the Prince /court / Ks.a whatever entity suits you . . .I am happy to represent KSA interests. period."
  • Raskin wrote that Epstein's "close relationship" with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman "became obvious" in the emails.
  • There were also indications Epstein coached the Kuwaiti emir on how to deal with Trump, the letter alleges. Epstein told a redacted sender in 2017 there was "much to do around the trump trip" and asked what "concrete" things the emir would "like to see happen from the US."

The letter also alleged Epstein advised former Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Barak, serving as his "sherpa through U.S.-Russia-Middle East diplomacy," and, "most disturbingly … appeared to give advice on how to handle President Trump to high-level officials in Russia."

  • Epstein appeared to have given Russia's late United Nations representative, Vitaly Churkin, advice on Trump, writing in 2018, "churkin was great. he understood trump after our conversations, it is not complex. he must be seen to get something its that simple."

What he's saying: "Jeffrey Epstein never registered as a foreign agent," Raskin wrote to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and and Jay Clayton, the U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York and Trump's nominee for director of national intelligence.

  • "Yet numerous recently released documents now show he acted aggressively on behalf of multiple foreign governments, including several with interests adverse to the United States, in order to influence policies of the first Trump Administration," he continued.
  • Raskin warned that Epstein's ties with foreign leaders posed a major national security liability and raised questions about what information he could have fed them about the president.

White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement to Axios, "Just as President Trump has said, he's been totally exonerated on anything relating to Epstein."

  • The DOJ did not immediately respond to Axios' request for comment.

What we're watching: Raskin gave the government until 5pm on Aug. 3 to provide records tied to Epstein's contacts with 19 nations — including Saudi Arabia, Israel, Kuwait, Russia and Qatar — as well as entities that may have been structured to avoid federal registration.

  • He also asked DOJ whether there have ever been Foreign Agents Registration Act investigations into Epstein and, if not, why it hadn't initiated any.
  • FARA requires what its name suggests: for foreign agents representing or doing advocacy work on behalf of foreign governments, people or organizations to register with the DOJ.

The bottom line: The files exposed the breadth of Epstein's far-reaching business and political web, prompting additional investigations and derailing careers.

  • And the web keeps unraveling, piling political and legal pressure on the administration to release withheld records.

Go deeper: Vance: "We did" mishandle Epstein files release

item.title: Trump administration consults unused 1930s law for tariff playbook
item.description:
The Trump administration keeps finding new ways to wage trade wars. As one novel tariff authority nears its expiration, it's testing another — threatening huge duties on Canadian goods under a never-before-used law.

Why it matters: The administration's willingness to test new trade authorities makes tariff policy harder for businesses, investors and foreign governments to handicap.

  • It's hard to know which will survive challenges in the courts, and over what time horizon there may be any resolution.
  • Even if individual tariffs are delayed or struck down, the administration can still use the threat of them to gain negotiating leverage while forcing companies to plan for higher costs.
  • It's unfolding even as renewed conflict in the Middle East pushes up energy costs and inflation risks — a backdrop that many economists thought would discourage the White House from opening new trade war fronts.

Driving the news: The White House says the tariffs respond to what it considers Canadian discrimination against U.S. autos, dairy and alcohol.

  • The administration invoked the never-before-used Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act and chose the maximum penalty allowed by the law: 50% duties, applied to roughly $20 billion of Canadian imports starting in August.
  • The duties would raise the average tariff rate on Canadian goods by roughly 2.3 percentage points, according to Karl Schamotta, Corpay's chief market strategist.

Between the lines: Section 338 is the latest legal authority the administration has reached for after courts earlier this year narrowed its emergency tariff powers.

  • The White House quickly replaced many of those invalidated tariffs with a 10% global levy under Section 122 — another never-before-used authority that happens to expire on Friday.
  • A federal trade court later struck down the Section 122 tariffs, though an appeals court allowed them to remain in effect pending appeal.
  • As that authority fades, the administration is replenishing its arsenal with more traditional trade laws. Top Trump trade official Jamieson Greer told CNBC on Tuesday morning to "expect to see some action soon" from a forced-labor investigation expected to produce tariffs on dozens of countries.

What to watch: Trade lawyers suggest the latest Canadian tariffs are also likely to face legal scrutiny — that is, if they take effect.

  • Peter Harrell, a visiting scholar at Georgetown Law's Institute of International Economic Law, wrote on X on Monday night that a potential challenger could argue that the administration bypassed the required procedures to impose the tariffs.
  • Ilya Somin — who helped successfully challenge President Trump's International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs — wrote that Congress "superseded" Section 338 through later trade legislation, leaving it without independent authority to impose new tariffs.

The other side: A senior administration official argued Section 338's "terms are clear" and said Canada's retaliation and preferential treatment of third countries "fits squarely" within the statute.

The bottom line: "The toothpaste is out of the tube. ... We would not be surprised to see [Section] 338 wielded against others," TD Cowen's Chris Krueger wrote in a client note Monday night, adding that "338 is the new IEEPA."

  • "Litigation [is] likely a question of when, not if, but that is likely a 2027 — or even 2028 — story. In the near term, elbows up across the board," Krueger added.

Trump stopped short of targeting some of Canada's most strategically important exports, exempting autos, lumber, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals and aircraft products from the new duties.

The intrigue: That's consistent with an administration trying to maximize negotiating leverage while limiting economic blowback, at a time when American voters are angry about inflation and the Iran war is keeping energy prices elevated.

  • The administration took the same tack last week, when it targeted Brazil, but left out key consumer goods like coffee and beef.
  • Still, the tariffs will apply to goods that qualify for duty-free treatment under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement, a departure from several earlier Trump tariff programs. Economists have argued that this carveout has shielded the U.S. from the worst of the Trump tariffs' potential inflationary effects.

The 30-day delay before the tariffs take effect leaves room for an off-ramp. A senior White House official stressed they remain open to negotiations, while Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney condemned the tariffs but likewise pledged to intensify talks before August.

  • A senior administration official said the measures are separate from Trump's previously threatened "wildfire tariffs," though the official said the White House is considering options on that front.
  • It's unclear which legal authority such tariffs would be imposed under.

item.title: Trump "public charge" rule resets health test for green cards
item.description:
The Trump administration formally laid the groundwork Monday to weigh immigrants' use of programs like Medicaid and CHIP in determining whether they should get permanent residency.

Why it matters: The policy shift could discourage immigrant families from seeking health coverage if it could put their legal status at risk, public health groups warn.

  • That could increase emergency room use, raise the cost of uncompensated care and widen health disparities, according to health policy experts.

Driving the news: The Department of Homeland Security published a new version of a "public charge" policy, which was expanded during President Trump's first term and then rolled back during the Biden years.

  • It overrides previous policies that limited which public benefits could disqualify immigrants from getting a green card, instead leaving decisions to the discretion of immigration officers on a case-by-case basis.
  • It also clarifies that a person's receipt of means-tested public benefits like Medicaid, CHIP and SNAP can be used to determine whether green card applicants would likely rely on government support.
  • The new policy "restores broader discretion for DHS officers to evaluate all pertinent facts and aligns with long-standing policy that aliens in the United States should be self-reliant and government benefits should not incentivize immigration," the rule states.

Between the lines: Public health and immigrant aid groups say the change could have a chilling effect.

  • A KFF-New York Times survey found 11% of immigrant adults said they stopped participating in public benefit programs last year due to immigration-related concerns, including about 4 in 10 (42%) who were likely undocumented and about 1 in 6 (17%) parents.

What they're saying: "This rule will exacerbate the pervasive fear and uncertainty among immigrant families," Andrew Racine, president of the American Academy of Pediatrics, said in a statement.

  • He said the change could "lead to children — including those who are U.S. citizens — not receiving vital health services and supports they need and are eligible for."
  • DHS estimated that more than $13 billion could be saved annually through the total reduction in transfer payments from federal and state governments.
  • It acknowledged the rule might result in reduced revenues for hospitals and other health providers participating in Medicaid, as well as for companies that make drugs or medical supplies.

Context: The federal government has long had policies for permanent residency to determine whether a person is likely to become overly reliant on public benefits. Certain immigrant groups like refugees and asylum-seekers are exempt.

  • The first Trump administration added most Medicaid services to public charge determinations. The Biden administration reversed the change, largely limiting determinations to cash welfare benefits.
  • But misconceptions about using health services lingered. About 25% of adults in families with both undocumented and legally present members avoided public benefits in 2022 because of green card concerns, per the Urban Institute.

What's ahead: The new policy will apply to applications for green cards made on or after Sept. 18.

item.title: Trump hosts Lebanon's president as Israel begins first pullback
item.description:
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun will meet President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday — a sitdown that comes as the Israeli military begins its first withdrawal from Lebanon under a U.S.-brokered agreement.

Why it matters: The Trump administration is politically invested in strengthening Aoun, isolating Hezbollah and pushing for an Israeli-Lebanese peace agreement.

  • Aoun, the first Lebanese president to visit the White House since 2009, wants political support from Trump, military assistance for the Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) and U.S. pressure on the Netanyahu government to continue its gradual withdrawal from areas it occupies in southern Lebanon.

Driving the news: On Monday, the State Department announced that Israel is starting to pull out of several villages it has occupied in southern Lebanon.

  • The limited withdrawal is part of a "pilot zone" agreement — essentially a test case for broader withdrawals — that the U.S. brokered between Israel and Lebanon last week in Rome.
  • It's the first Israeli pullback from Lebanese territory since it launched its ground invasion in March in response to Hezbollah attacks.
  • A U.S. official said the LAF will be responsible for these zones. It will clear them of Hezbollah weapons and infrastructure, conduct verified clearance operations and secure the areas so Hezbollah cannot use them for military activity.
  • The U.S. is still negotiating with Israel and Lebanon over who will verify that the LAF cleared the areas so that the next phase of the withdrawal can take place, Israeli officials said.
  • A senior Israeli official said the Israeli Defense Forces [IDF] withdrawal started on Tuesday morning local time.
  • The IDF official called the initial withdrawal "a test for the Lebanese Armed Forces' sovereignty in the three pilot villages."

What they're saying: "The establishment of these initial pilot zones represents real progress on the ground," a U.S. official said.

  • The official stressed that the Trump administration hopes the pilot zones will "build confidence" between Israel and Lebanon and "create the momentum needed to expand the process."
  • "We expect all parties to respect this process. Any attempt by Hezbollah to derail, infiltrate, or re-arm in these zones would seriously harm the prospects for reconstruction and peace, and the interests of the Lebanese people," the U.S. official said.

The big picture: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the lead U.S. official on Israeli-Lebanese matters, met Aoun on Sunday before leaving on a trip to Asia.

  • Rubio told reporters the best way to defeat Hezbollah is by strengthening the Lebanese government and the LAF so that they will be able to replace the Shia militia. He wants the Lebanese government to provide security and economic prosperity to the population in southern Lebanon and the rest of the country.
  • "This is not just about military stuff. This is about how can we attract more U.S. and international investment into Lebanon," Rubio said. "And I think President Aoun will be able to return to Lebanon and say to the people of Lebanon that he is the one that's getting positive results for the people ... not Hezbollah, not Iran."

What to watch: Trump and Aoun have never met in person, but they have held two phone calls in recent months that U.S. and Lebanese officials say were very positive.

  • Trump has become deeply invested in the Lebanese issue over the last few months. He is urging Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to complete the withdrawal of the Israeli military from southern Lebanon.
  • But both Trump and Aoun recognize that a real acceleration of the pullout and a possible breakthrough toward a lasting peace deal can't happen until after the Israeli elections in October.

item.title: Inside a group's push for whites-only "heritage communities"
item.description:
A white separatist group is openly planning a network of whites-only settlements across rural America that aim to withstand legal challenges by operating essentially as private clubs.

Why it matters: The communities are launching in the Ozarks, Appalachia and the South as the Trump administration has backed off prosecuting many civil rights cases.

  • They're also surfacing at a time when the nation is becoming increasingly diverse, and as the racist "great replacement theory" has become a staple of far-right politics in recent years.

The big picture: For nearly three years, state and federal authorities have largely watched from the sidelines as the separatist group Return to the Land (RTTL) unveiled plans and took applications for residents.

  • Those plans amount to intentionally challenging the Fair Housing Act of 1968, which banned discrimination in housing. They've been inspired partly by recent decisions by the conservative-led U.S. Supreme Court and the Trump administration's reversal of much of President Lyndon Johnson's civil rights legacy.

Catch up quick: RTTL established a 160-acre, whites-only community near Ravenden, Ark., in late 2023.

  • Using an AI-generated corporate framework discovered on 4chan, the founders structured the community as a "private membership association" linked to a for-profit LLC, according to court documents.
  • They boast that this dual structure creates a loophole that allows them to bypass requirements of the Fair Housing Act and explicitly filter their neighbors by race and religion.
  • The group said work has begun on a second site in Arkansas, and it's planning more projects in the Ozarks, the South and Appalachia.

Zoom in: RTTL's website states that it "help[s] groups of our members form European heritage communities."

  • It seeks to create "a decentralized movement" and aims "to create many independent and fully functioning parallel societies."
  • The group also helps people and groups with "common ancestry" purchase rural land.

By the numbers: About 40 people, including roughly a dozen home-schooled children, now live in the compound in Ravenden on land purchased for $237,000.

  • An application fee for the private membership association costs $25, after which members undergo background checks and share information about their ethnic background.
  • RTTL had raised more than $200,000 as of May 2026, including $105,158 for additional chapters and $94,401 for legal-framework research, court records show.

In interviews with media outlets and alt-right leaning influencers, RTTL founder Eric Orwoll has referenced Hitler, said the community had a "pretty good radar" for excluding Jews and called himself a "white identitarian."

  • "You're going to get Hitler 2.0 when you deserve him. He's not just going to show up to a people that aren't putting in the work themselves. We have to put in that work," Orwoll said in a video posted on X.
  • RTTL and its lawyer did not respond to Axios' request for comment.

Yes, but: RTTL is facing a federal lawsuit after a practicing Christian with Russian-Jewish ancestry applied to purchase property at the group's whites-only settlement near Ravenden and was rejected after disclosing her heritage, her marriage to a Black man and her biracial children.

  • The lawsuit alleges that RTTL's founders want to illegally build small whites-only neighborhoods and scale them quickly.
  • Founders also want to develop settlements of up to 50,000 people and eventually "cities of thousands," the suit alleges.

Between the lines: RTTL's attempt to exploit a conservative shift in the judiciary is weighed down by a highly flawed legal defense and longstanding federal statutes, Stacy Seicshnaydre, a Tulane University Law School professor, tells Axios.

  • Seicshnaydre said the Civil Rights Act of 1866 is the group's most formidable legal hurdle, noting that the Reconstruction-era statute has zero exemptions and prohibits all racial and cultural discrimination. Much of the language of that law is reflected in the 13th Amendment.
  • "I would be very surprised to see the court take this step because it would be quite a radical one, and would unravel just very fundamental principles of property rights and inclusion," Seicshnaydre said.

What they're saying: "We abhor racism, and it has no place in our society," Jeff LeMaster, a spokesperson for Arkansas Attorney General Tim Griffin, told Axios in a statement.

  • "Regarding Return to the Land, we began looking into the development after receiving complaints last year. We have no updates we can share at this time."

The U.S. Department of Justice did not respond to Axios.

What we're watching: If the federal lawsuit reaches the discovery phase, it could expose RTTL's membership rules, land agreements and plans for additional communities — details the group has largely kept private.
item.content:encoded:
A white separatist group is openly planning a network of whites-only settlements across rural America that aim to withstand legal challenges by operating essentially as private clubs.

Why it matters: The communities are launching in the Ozarks, Appalachia and the South as the Trump administration has backed off prosecuting many civil rights cases.

  • They're also surfacing at a time when the nation is becoming increasingly diverse, and as the racist "great replacement theory" has become a staple of far-right politics in recent years.

The big picture: For nearly three years, state and federal authorities have largely watched from the sidelines as the separatist group Return to the Land (RTTL) unveiled plans and took applications for residents.

  • Those plans amount to intentionally challenging the Fair Housing Act of 1968, which banned discrimination in housing. They've been inspired partly by recent decisions by the conservative-led U.S. Supreme Court and the Trump administration's reversal of much of President Lyndon Johnson's civil rights legacy.

  • Using an AI-generated corporate framework discovered on 4chan, the founders structured the community as a "private membership association" linked to a for-profit LLC, according to court documents.
  • They boast that this dual structure creates a loophole that allows them to bypass requirements of the Fair Housing Act and explicitly filter their neighbors by race and religion.
  • The group said work has begun on a second site in Arkansas, and it's planning more projects in the Ozarks, the South and Appalachia.

Zoom in: RTTL's website states that it "help[s] groups of our members form European heritage communities."

  • It seeks to create "a decentralized movement" and aims "to create many independent and fully functioning parallel societies."
  • The group also helps people and groups with "common ancestry" purchase rural land.

  • An application fee for the private membership association costs $25, after which members undergo background checks and share information about their ethnic background.

What they're saying: "We abhor racism, and it has no place in our society," Jeff LeMaster, a spokesperson for Arkansas Attorney General Tim Griffin, told Axios in a statement.

item.title: Iran mediators push new ceasefire as Trump eyes all-out war
item.description:
President Trump is nearing a decisive fork in the Iran war, with U.S. and Israeli officials envisioning only two viable endgames:

  • Option 1: Pursue a new 10-day ceasefire aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Option 2: Launch a massive joint military campaign with Israel to force Tehran's capitulation.

Why it matters: Trump hasn't made a decision yet. But officials believe the next few days will determine which path prevails — with little room left for the grinding, open-ended conflict now paralyzing the Gulf.

Driving the news: Qatar, Egypt, Pakistan and other regional mediators have presented the U.S. and Iran with a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire, two regional sources with direct knowledge of the effort told Axios.

  • The Trump administration is exploring the proposal and has urged Israel to avoid steps that could close the diplomatic window, U.S. officials said.
  • But Washington is simultaneously preparing for the talks to fail. The U.S. military has sent dozens of fighter jets and refueling aircraft to the region in recent days, assembling the forces needed for a major escalation.
  • Israeli sources said the IDF is on high alert and preparing for a possible expansion of the war into a full-scale, coordinated campaign within days.

The big picture: Ten straight nights of U.S. bombing have failed to break Iran's grip on the Strait of Hormuz, dragging Trump deeper into a war he has repeatedly declared won.

  • At least three U.S. service members have been killed in the renewed fighting. Trump said Monday he had ordered the Pentagon to make Iran pay "many times over" for every American death.
  • Oil briefly broke $90 a barrel this weekend as Iran widened its strikes on commercial ships and Gulf infrastructure. Gas prices are rising again, compounding the domestic backlash to an already unpopular war.

Zoom in: Regional mediators are once again racing to interrupt a cycle of retaliation that grows deadlier, costlier and harder to contain with each new strike.

  • The new ceasefire proposal would halt the fighting, reopen both shipping lanes through the Strait of Hormuz, and give Washington and Tehran room to salvage their collapsing memorandum of understanding (MOU).
  • "We told the U.S. and Iran that we propose a cooling-off period," one of the regional sources said.

State of play: Neither Trump nor Iran has accepted the proposal, and both sides appear determined to improve their leverage through further strikes before committing to a ceasefire.

  • In the meantime, the Iran-backed Houthis in Yemen have declared a maritime blockade on Saudi ports, opening another front against the Gulf's energy infrastructure and shipping lanes.
  • Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has warned that Israel will answer any Iranian attack with massive strikes, raising the risk of a wider war stretching from the Gulf to the Red Sea and Israel.

Behind the scenes: Regional sources said mediators are trying to build on a proposal discussed by Oman, Iran and Qatar in Muscat, Oman, on July 11, when the U.S. demanded Tehran publicly commit to keeping the Strait of Hormuz open.

  • Those talks ended without an agreement, and Iran attacked commercial ships in the strait shortly afterward — triggering the U.S. bombing campaign that has continued every night since.
  • The proposal would fully reopen both Hormuz shipping lanes: the southern route through Omani waters, free of Iranian attacks, and the northern route through Iranian waters, free of the U.S. blockade.

During the 10-day ceasefire, the U.S. and Iran would negotiate a longer-term arrangement governing passage through the strait.

  • One option would allow Iran to collect "reasonable service fees" for maritime security, environmental protection and other services, according to one regional source.
  • The source pointed to the Strait of Malacca — where Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore charge vessels for specific services rather than a blanket transit fee — as a possible model.
  • Another idea would direct the fees into a joint fund administered with the involvement of the International Maritime Organization.

Officials involved in the mediation include Qatari Prime Minister Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani, Qatari envoy Ali Al-Thawadi, Pakistani army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir, Egyptian Foreign Minister Badr Abdelatty and Omani Foreign Minister Badr al-Busaidi.

The intrigue: One regional source said the ceasefire effort was gaining momentum Saturday, before an Iranian missile strike in Jordan killed at least two U.S. service members and prompted Washington to pull back.

  • A senior U.S. official confirmed that talks are continuing but said Trump has yet to finish retaliating.
  • "The president is focused on making Iran pay for its violations of the MOU and its continued acts of terrorism in the Strait of Hormuz," the official told Axios.
  • "In addition, the president will make Iran pay for the recent deaths of U.S. soldiers. These devastating blows will continue until the president deems otherwise."

What they're saying: Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei confirmed Monday that mediators were working to prevent further escalation.

  • "The proposals conveyed through the mediators have been received by us, but at this stage I prefer not to discuss their details," Baghaei said at an Iranian press briefing.
  • He added that Iran's armed forces would continue to "respond with strength and crush the source of U.S. aggression."

What to watch: It's unclear whether Trump has been fully briefed on the ceasefire framework — or whether he would accept another temporary ceasefire without a clear path toward a lasting settlement.

  • The president has voiced deep frustration with Tehran since the ceasefire collapsed, calling Iran's leaders "sick people" and dismissing further negotiations as a "waste of time."
  • One regional source said the White House may continue bombing Iran for several more days to avenge the killing of the U.S. service members in Jordan before seriously considering the proposal.

The bottom line: "We are trying to work out a sustainable solution," one regional source said. "The Iranians can't dictate the terms, but they also can't be ignored."

item.title: U.S. to slap 50% tariffs on Canadian goods
item.description:
The Trump administration said Monday that it will impose an additional 50% tariff on certain Canadian goods — including hockey sticks, wine and cement — accusing Canada of discriminating against U.S. exports.

Why it matters: The move ratchets up tensions in an increasingly bitter trade dispute between two of the world's closest economic partners.

Zoom out: The tariffs, which take effect next month, will be imposed under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930 — a never-before-used authority. The duties cover roughly $20 billion in annual Canadian imports.

  • A senior administration official said the tariffs were not the wildfire tariffs that President Trump had earlier threatened, though the official added that options on that front remain under consideration.

Zoom in: The administration says that Canada is one of only two countries — alongside China — to retaliate against Trump's earlier tariffs, pointing to provinces that pulled U.S. liquor from store shelves.

  • Officials also cited auto policies they say favor Canadian production and dairy rules they argue give European cheese an advantage over American imports.

The backdrop: Trump threatened to impose additional Canadian tariffs last week in a Truth Social post, saying that costs related to the wildfire smoke must be added to the tariffs.

  • Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney were seen together on Sunday at the World Cup final match in East Rutherford, N.J.
  • Trump told reporters the two men talked about potential action in response to the wildfires while noting their "good relationship."

The big picture: This marks another escalation in U.S.-Canada tensions after the Trump administration declined to extend the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement, leaving the continent's trade pact in limbo.

  • Unlike previous Trump tariffs on Canada, the new duties apply to goods that qualify for duty-free treatment under the USMCA.
  • Economists have found that the trade deal has been critical in limiting the tariff impact on consumer prices.
  • The exemptions spare some of Canada's most strategically important exports, including potash, critical minerals and most energy products, limiting the potential fallout for U.S. industry.

The other side: In a statement posted on X, Carney called the tariffs "in direct violation" of the USMCA and pledged to intensify negotiations in the coming weeks.

  • The Canadian Chamber of Commerce called the tariffs a "regrettable escalation" and urged both countries to use the month before they take effect to advance negotiations.

The intrigue: This year's Supreme Court decision limiting Trump's emergency tariff powers forced the administration to search for other legal avenues to impose its trade agenda.

  • That includes the administration's temporary 10% global tariffs under Section 122 of the Trade Act — an authority that expires later this week.

Editor's note: This story was updated with additional details.

item.title: Senate watches the House on reconciliation 3.0
item.description:
Senate Republicans are warming to the idea of using a third reconciliation package to replenish the Pentagon's depleted weapons stocks.

Why it matters: They still view reconciliation 3.0 as Plan B.

  • For now, though, they're willing to let House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) attempt the legislative equivalent of a triple axel: passing a rule, a short-term government funding bill and a budget resolution to unlock reconciliation.
  • "The tale of the tape is going to be what the House can do," Sen. Shelley Moore Capito (R-W.Va.) said.
  • "Can the House pass it? I don't think that's a done deal. It's not offset, so I think there's going to be some troubled water there."

But Republican senators aren't slamming the door on Johnson's plan, looking to keep Senate options open.

  • "It's pretty clear that [Senate Minority Leader Chuck] Schumer would like to play shutdown politics," Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said. "So I don't have any problem with using reconciliation for that."
  • "But beyond that, it gets pretty narrow, what I'd be willing to support."

Driving the news: President Trump is ramping up pressure on House Republicans to support the budget resolution ahead of a pivotal week on Capitol Hill.

  • "I am asking all House Republicans to VOTE YES this week on the Budget Resolution, which is an important first step towards getting a Budget Bill," Trump wrote Monday on Truth Social.
  • Office of Management and Budget Director Russ Vought will try to close the deal at the Republican conference Tuesday.
  • The move is a clear signal the administration wants a fallback plan if the normal appropriations process isn't done by Oct. 1 — which looks increasingly likely.

Meanwhile, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will testify before the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday to defend the administration's supplemental funding request, including tens of billions of dollars to replenish munitions used in Iran.

Between the lines: Senate Majority Leader John Thune is cautioning that reconciliation 3.0 still faces a steep climb, even if it clears the House.

  • "Easier said than done," Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters.
  • And not every Senate Republican is on board.
  • "I am not a big fan of reconciliation whether it's 1.0, 2.0 or 3.0," Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) said.

Zoom in: The White House has sent a list of spending anomalies to appropriators — a necessary step for Congress and OMB to advance a short-term continuing resolution.

  • The move is a clear signal that the administration expects Congress to rely on a stopgap spending bill to avert a shutdown.
  • That remains senators' preferred path.
  • "I'd love a clean CR to get us through the election," Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) said.

Zoom out: Vice President Vance's private appeal to House Republicans last week did little to change the political math. Johnson faces a difficult challenge.

  • The skepticism extends beyond the House. Some Senate Republicans want a larger package with more defense funding, while others insist that at least some of the roughly $95 billion in new spending be offset.
  • The House proposal includes about $60 billion to replenish munitions and weapons stocks depleted by U.S. operations against Iran.

The bottom line: The Senate is waiting on the House while pursuing a two-track strategy: keep appropriations moving while keeping reconciliation in reserve if negotiations break down.

  • "We either need to keep appropriations moving," Sen. John Hoeven (R-N.D.) told Axios, "or it's reconciliation 3.0."

item.title: Scoop: Rep. Chuck Edwards gifted pink and blue handguns to young female staffers
item.description:
Rep. Chuck Edwards (R-N.C.) gave two young female staffers each a personalized handgun as a present while they worked in his office in 2024, according to two sources with direct knowledge of the gifts.

Why it matters: The guns, and a surprise musical performance organized by the congressman, are additional examples of Edwards allegedly singling out the young female staffers for unusually personal attention, conduct that is now at the center of a House Ethics Committee investigation into alleged sexual harassment and creating a hostile work environment.

  • Edwards gave one staffer a blue handgun engraved with her name and the other staffer a pink handgun. Both gifts occurred in North Carolina.
  • A lawyer for one of the former staffers told Axios that she "only awkwardly accepted the gift for fear of reprisal against her employment but later legally disposed of it because she did not want it in her possession."
  • A member buying gifts for staffers is not explicitly against House rules.

Edwards was a licensed firearms dealer until November 2024. He previously owned a firearms business called Second Amendment Gun Traders in Hendersonville, North Carolina.

  • He touts an "A" rating from the National Rifle Association on his campaign website.

In a statement to Axios, Edwards said: "I have taught several staff members to shoot and handle weapons, and in some cases, I provided them with their own. Before being elected to Congress, I was a licensed firearm dealer. I'm a strong believer in teaching men and women about firearm safety and self-defense. Firearms are useful tools for home and self-defense when held by law-abiding citizens."

  • Three sources told Axios that they had never seen or heard of Edwards giving other staffers firearms or teaching other staffers how to use a firearm.

Axios has previously reported that Edwards singled out the two young female staffers in their 20s for attention that multiple sources described as inappropriate, including handwritten correspondence, personal gifts, drinking outings, casino trips and promotions.

  • The congressman has denied wrongdoing in response to Axios' previous reporting about his alleged misconduct.
  • One of the former staffers declined to discuss her interactions with Edwards when contacted by Axios. Axios will not name the staffers due to the sensitive nature of the allegations.

Edwards also arranged a surprise musical performance during a personal birthday party for one of the staffers while she worked in his office, according to three sources familiar with the event.

  • In spring 2025, Edwards hired a singer to perform the staffer's favorite Ed Sheeran songs during the staffer's birthday party at Salazar in Washington, D.C.

The sources described the staffer as being embarrassed by the performance, which they said was arranged without her prior knowledge.

  • Edwards did not attend the party. Instead, he coordinated the surprise through one of the staffer's friends, whom he had met while visiting the staffer in the hospital when she had appendicitis, one of the sources told Axios.
  • Axios previously reported Edwards left Capitol Hill during the workday to visit the staffer at the hospital, according to two sources.
  • Edwards didn't answer a question from Axios about the musical performance he allegedly organized.

Catch up quick: Edwards wrote one staffer a flattering poem that he read aloud — while choking up and becoming teary-eyed — at her office-wide going-away party, four sources told Axios.

  • He also told that staffer that she had "written a complex chapter in my heart" shortly before she was set to leave his office in a three-page handwritten letter reviewed by Axios.
  • Edwards gave the staffer a custom puzzle revealing an image of Adam Sandler with a handwritten note inviting her to attend one of Sandler's comedy shows with him, according to a photo viewed by Axios and three sources with knowledge of the gift.

During the fall 2025 government shutdown, Edwards vacationed with the staffer in Las Vegas after she left his office, where he arranged for flowers to be waiting in her Bellagio hotel room, according to two sources familiar with the trip and receipts reviewed by Axios. The female staffer told Edwards the flowers made her uncomfortable, according to the sources.

  • One of the staffers complained about Edwards' behavior and was worried about retaliation from the congressman, according to text messages reviewed by Axios and four sources familiar with the interactions.

Zoom out: The ethics investigation could complicate Edwards' reelection prospects, though investigations typically take months, if not years, to complete.

  • Democrats have made Edwards' western North Carolina district a top target in their effort to win back the House.
  • Edwards' Democratic challenger, Jamie Ager, outraised him by roughly $1.3 million in the latest fundraising quarter.

Edwards and his wife, Teresa, announced last week that they sold all six of their McDonald's franchises to "devote even greater attention to public service." Financial terms of the sale were not disclosed.

  • Edwards also recently removed references to President Trump from his campaign website after previously highlighting the president and seeking his endorsement.

item.title: Nordone to launch bid for Graham's Senate seat
item.description:
Sen. Darline Graham Nordone (R-S.C.) confirmed on Monday that she's jumping into the GOP race for her late brother Lindsey Graham's Senate seat.

Why it matters: President Trump endorsed Nordone last week, giving her a boost ahead of the filing period for the seat, which opens Tuesday.

What they're saying: "I'm in," Nordone said when asked in a Fox News interview clip posted on X Monday.

  • "It is a tremendous amount of pressure," she said, referring to both her decision to run and Trump's endorsement.
  • "I'll continue to say I've been praying a lot, talking to my family. But with the love of my family, with the support of his staff, I know I'm a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey. He cared about the people of this state. I've worked for almost 30 years for the people of this state."
  • The full interview will air Monday night on Fox News' "Hannity."

Catch up quick: South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster (R) appointed Nordone last week to replace her brother after his sudden death.

  • She has never held public office and previously worked to help people with disabilities find employment.

Zoom out: The filing period for the special election closes July 28.

  • South Carolinians will vote in an Aug. 11 Republican primary, with the winner advancing to the November ballot to face Democratic candidate Annie Andrews.
  • The winner of November's contest will hold Graham's former seat for the full six-year term.
  • Rep. Ralph Norman (R-S.C.) confirmed on Saturday that he was hopping into the race, as did Rep. Russell Fry on Monday (R-S.C.). Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) said she will not run for the seat on Monday, despite "strongly considering" it before Trump endorsed Nordone.

Editor's note: This story has been updated with additional information throughout.

item.title: Cyclospora investigation still points to recalled lettuce, FDA says
item.description:
The FDA said Monday it still thinks Taylor Farms lettuce is likely behind a Cyclospora outbreak, even after pulling back earlier test results linking the produce to the parasite.

Why it matters: Tracking the source of the diarrhea-causing illness presents a steep challenge for health officials and suppliers, while news of a false positive risks confusing consumers about which recalled products to avoid.

  • At least 1,644 people have fallen ill, though officials believe the true number is likely much higher.
  • Taylor Farms has voluntarily recalled shredded iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico in connection with the outbreak.

The big picture: Laboratory results are only one piece of foodborne outbreak investigations, which also rely on tracing illnesses and food shipments.

  • The sample that was initially flagged as positive over the weekend — before the FDA walked back that determination — was not part of the current recall, acting Deputy Commissioner for Food Donald Prater explained in a Monday call with reporters.
  • The sample's re-evaluation "does not alter the data supporting the outbreak advisory that prompted the current voluntary recall," he added.
  • The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is still advising people to avoid recalled products — including at Taco Bell, which says it has removed the affected lettuce from its restaurants.

Catch up quick: The FDA turned heads with its update that a sample of Taylor Farms lettuce from Mexico that had originally tested positive for Cyclospora was re-reviewed and "should be considered a false positive."

  • Taylor Farms said in a social media statement that the "FDA apologized to us" and that the agency had "made a mistake."
  • However, in a post, the FDA clarified that its traceback investigation of the surge in cases was still converging on shredded iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms locations in central Mexico.
  • An FDA official said Monday that while the agency provided information to explain the circumstances to Taylor Farms, which produces salads and other produce, it did not offer an official apology.

Between the lines: FDA officials have stressed that it's challenging to track Cyclospora in food, noting restrictions like the short shelf-life of fresh produce and the lengthy incubation period before symptoms show up.

  • "Test results are difficult to obtain, and most of the time, our traceback and epidemiologic information are what allows us to determine the source of the outbreak," Prater explained.

What we're watching: Acting FDA Commissioner Kyle Diamantas stressed Monday that the investigation is ongoing, "including into other potential sources and commodities for other clusters of illnesses."

Go deeper: Gut-wrecking stomach bug mysteriously surges in U.S.

item.title: Exclusive: House Republicans outraise Democrats in June
item.description:
The House Republicans' campaign arm brought in $17.6 million in June, leaving them with $92.7 million in cash on hand, Axios has learned.

Why it matters: The National Republican Congressional Campaign Committee is entering the final midterm stretch with a cash advantage over House Democrats, giving GOP leaders another talking point as they try to defy the historical trend that typically favors the party out of power.

  • The NRCC outraised its counterpart, the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, by $1.2 million in June.
  • That brought the NRCC's cash on hand to $92.7 million, versus $79 million for the DCCC.

Yes, but: The DCCC outraised the NRCC in the full second quarter of 2026, bringing in $37.4 million, compared to $35.4 million for NRCC.

What they're saying: "Republicans are building a campaign machine to win. Democrats are still holding clown show socialist auditions for who gets to lose in November," NRCC Spokesman Mike Marinella told Axios in a statement.

  • DCCC Chair Suzan DelBene said in a statement Monday that the group's "strong quarter of fundraising combined with the incredible results of our Frontliners and challengers show that across the battlefield, Democrats are assembling people-powered campaigns ready to win in November and make Hakeem Jeffries the next speaker of the House."

Zoom in: The NRCC's fundraising edge hasn't translated to individual candidates.

  • Twenty-five GOP incumbents — and Rep. Kevin Kiley (I-Calif.) — were outraised by a Democratic challenger in the second quarter of 2026, according to Punchbowl News.

Zoom out: The Supreme Court's ruling last month allowing unlimited coordinated spending between national party committees and candidates supercharges the NRCC's fundraising advantage.

  • Instead of being constrained by coordination limits, the committee can now funnel more of its financial edge directly into competitive House races.

item.title: Paramount Skydance-Warner Bros. Discovery merger paused by federal judge
item.description:
A federal judge has paused Paramount Skydance's takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery in response to a lawsuit last week from a dozen state attorneys general looking to block the deal on antitrust grounds.

Why it matters: The delay represents the most significant legal challenge related to the merger globally to-date.

  • While some foreign regulators, including the U.K.'s Competition and Markets Authority, are still reviewing the deal, none have issued an order that independently bars Paramount and WBD from closing.

Zoom in: On Monday, California District Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín issued a 14-day restraining order that prevents Paramount and WBD from closing their deal.

  • The judge argued it was in the public's best interest to issue the pause, because the states raised antitrust questions in their lawsuit that warrant further review.

Between the lines: The states have also requested a preliminary injunction to block the deal until the judge rules on the merits of their case.

  • In her order, Martínez-Olguín set a preliminary injunction hearing for Aug. 3 to determine whether the temporary restraining order needs to be extended. That date could be delayed if the parties agree.
  • The judge notes that the temporary restraining order can be extended for good cause. The order could be extended to as long as 28 days.

Catch up quick: The coalition of states that sued to block the merger last week asked for an injunction that would not allow the two companies to close the deal until after the judicial process concludes.

  • The states and Paramount both presented their arguments to the judge last week.
  • The lawsuit, which was led by California Attorney General Rob Bonta, argued the merger would reduce competition among movie studios and would have too much market power over cable channels.

What they're saying: "This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day," Bonta said.

  • "Consolidation in the film and television industry not only leads to higher prices, but it also leads to fewer opportunities for important stories to come to life and fewer ways for audiences to encounter stories, ideas and perspectives beyond their own experiences."

The other side: A Paramount spokesperson said in a statement, "We are confident the evidence will demonstrate that the State AGs' antitrust arguments are without merit as their alleged markets and claims of anticompetitive effects are without any basis in modern market realities."

  • "This merger is lawful, pro-competitive, and will benefit consumers, creators, workers, and the entertainment industry," they said.

Zoom out: Not only does the pause create uncertainty for Paramount and WBD shareholders as they look to get the deal over the finish line, but any delay could also cost Paramount hundreds of millions of dollars.

  • Paramount promised WBD shareholders a 25-cent-per-share ticking fee for every quarter the deal doesn't close by Sept. 30, totaling around $650 million per quarter.

What's next: Paramount said it hopes to close the deal by the end of September.

Editor's note: This story was updated with Paramount's statement.

item.title: Troy Jackson poised to seize Maine baton after Platner's exit
item.description:
Democrats are rallying around former Maine Senate President Troy Jackson (D) in the quest to unseat Republican Sen. Susan Collins this fall after Graham Platner's embattled campaign crashed, burned and ended.

Why it matters: Jackson's expected coronation is another win for the left flank of the Democratic party, which is lining up behind another progressive candidate as their best shot at flipping one of the most important seats to win back Senate control.

Driving the news: Jackson all but locked up the nomination last weekend after Democrats across the state overwhelmingly selected his preferred delegate slate ahead of Saturday's convention.

  • "We're well on our way to getting the government that we fucking deserve," Jackson said in a video to supporters.
  • Top contenders in the crowded race have since ended their campaigns. Nirav Shah, Dan Kleban, and Jordan Wood all backed Jackson.
  • The nominee will replace the scandal-plagued Platner, who was accused of sexual assault — an allegation he denies.

Catch up quick: Jackson, whose campaign describes him as a fifth-generation logger and "lifelong fighter for workers' rights," finished third in June's Democratic primary for governor.

  • His campaign priorities include passing Medicare for all, cutting off tax dollars to Israel's military, abolishing ICE and passing a federal right to abortion.
  • Progressive kingmaker Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) endorsed Jackson in the gubernatorial race.

What's next: Saturday's convention of 601 delegates from the state's 16 counties will make the new Democratic candidate official.

  • "The convention will be fair, representative, and as transparent as possible as we all come together in service of our ultimate goal: defeat Susan Collins and win this Senate seat," Maine Democratic Party Chair Charlie Dingman said in a statement.
  • Votes will be cast in rounds until one candidate reaches a majority, according to the party. The delegates are not pledged to any specific candidate, but a majority have reportedly committed to backing Jackson.

Friction point: Jackson may glide through his party's nomination process, but Republicans have been preparing a massive ad blitz against Platner's replacement.

Go deeper: The Trump effect: Why Dems embraced a Platner time bomb

item.title: Scoop: Trump AI security agency head resigns
item.description:
Chris Fall, the director of the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months after taking over the federal AI testing institute, the Commerce Department confirmed on Monday.

Why it matters: The abrupt departure comes as the Trump administration grapples with how to deploy AI safely and the agency hashes out standards.

  • Commerce spokesperson Benno Kass confirmed Fall's resignation to Axios.

Context: Fall was appointed in April to lead Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation after the administration reorganized the agency formerly known as the U.S. AI Safety Institute.

  • CAISI is responsible for developing AI testing and evaluation capabilities and supporting standards for advanced AI systems.

What they're saying: "Following Chris's departure, NIST Director Dr. Arvind Raman will continue to oversee CAISI and will serve as Acting CAISI Director," Commerce spokesperson Kristen Eichamer said in a statement to Axios.

  • Raman, a former Purdue University engineering dean, was sworn in as the director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology on June 30.

What we're watching: While Raman will serve as acting director, the office will remain without permanent leadership as the administration debates its next steps on AI standards and oversight.

What's next: The Commerce Department expects to announce a new director in the coming weeks.

  • A Commerce official said Fall's appointment was always intended to be temporary and that Raman has been reviewing candidates over the past several weeks.

Editor's note: This story has been updated with a statement from the Department of Commerce.
item.content:encoded:
Chris Fall, the director of the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months after taking over the federal AI testing institute, the Commerce Department confirmed on Monday.

Why it matters: The abrupt departure comes as the Trump administration grapples with how to deploy AI safely and the agency hashes out standards.

  • Commerce spokesperson Benno Kass confirmed Fall's resignation to Axios.

Context: Fall was appointed in April to lead Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation after the administration reorganized the agency formerly known as the U.S. AI Safety Institute.

  • CAISI is responsible for developing AI testing and evaluation capabilities and supporting standards for advanced AI systems.

What they're saying: "Following Chris's departure, NIST Director Dr. Arvind Raman will continue to oversee CAISI and will serve as Acting CAISI Director," Commerce spokesperson Kristen Eichamer said in a statement to Axios.

  • Raman, a former Purdue University engineering dean, was sworn in as the director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology on June 30.

What we're watching: While Raman will serve as acting director, the office will remain without permanent leadership as the administration debates its next steps on AI standards and oversight.

What's next: The Commerce Department expects to announce a new director in the coming weeks.

  • A Commerce official said Fall's appointment was always intended to be temporary and that Raman has been reviewing candidates over the past several weeks.

Editor's note: This story has been updated with a statement from the Department of Commerce.
